documents.text:
N-400 Naturalization Application Guide + Checklist
Overview
Form N-400, Application for Naturalization, is used by lawful permanent residents (green card holders) to apply for U.S. citizenship. Applicants must meet certain eligibility requirements including continuous residence, physical presence, and good moral character.
Instructions Summary
This section provides a simplified overview of the main sections in Form N-400:
Part 1: Eligibility – Select your eligibility category (e.g., 5 years LPR, 3 years married to U.S. citizen).
Part 2: Information About You – Name, address, date of birth, Social Security Number, and A-Number.
Part 3–6: Additional Personal Info – Work history, travel outside the U.S., marital history.
Part 7–11: Family Information – Info on spouse, children, parents, and prior marriages.
Part 12: Additional Information – Answer Yes/No questions regarding background, crimes, affiliations.
Part 13: Applicant’s Signature – Certify and sign the application.
Parts 14–18: Interpreter, Preparer, and Additional Information sections.
Completion Checklist
Use this checklist to ensure your N-400 application packet is complete:
Filing Tips
- Ensure continuous residence and physical presence requirements are met.
- If you’ve had any criminal issues, consult an attorney before filing.
- Be honest and complete with all responses, especially Yes/No questions in Part 12.
- Prepare for the civics and English test unless applying for an exemption.

Immigration Form Filing Flowchart (Bonus)
Use this simplified flowchart to visualize which USCIS forms are commonly filed together based on the case type. This tool is helpful for new paralegals and legal assistants to understand how various immigration filings interconnect.
Common Filing Combinations

I-864 Completion Guide + Checklist
Overview
Form I-864, Affidavit of Support, is required for most family-based and some employment-based immigrants to demonstrate that they will not become a public charge in the United States. The petitioner (and in some cases a joint sponsor) must agree to financially support the intending immigrant.
Instructions Summary
Here’s a plain-language breakdown of the key sections of Form I-864:
Part 1: Basis for Filing Affidavit – Select whether you are the petitioner, joint sponsor, or substitute sponsor.
Part 2: Information About the Principal Immigrant – Include name, A-Number, and relationship to sponsor.
Part 3: Information About the Immigrants You Are Sponsoring – Add all beneficiaries you are sponsoring.
Part 4: Sponsor’s Information – Include your citizenship status, address, Social Security number.
Part 5: Sponsor’s Household Size – Count yourself, dependents, and any sponsored immigrants.
Part 6: Sponsor’s Income and Employment – Provide most recent income and current job details.
Part 7: Use of Assets to Supplement Income – Optional if income doesn’t meet guidelines.
Part 8: Sponsor’s Contract – Signature section affirming legal responsibility.
Parts 9–11: Interpreter, Preparer, and Additional Information sections.
Completion Checklist
Use this checklist to ensure the I-864 packet is complete:
Filing Tips
- Use IRS tax transcript if tax return documents are unavailable or lengthy.
- All sponsors must sign the form in ink; electronic signatures are not accepted.
- If using assets, ensure they are readily convertible and documented.
- Sponsors are financially responsible until the immigrant becomes a citizen or earns 40 work quarters.

I-131 Completion Guide + Checklist
Overview
Form I-131, Application for Travel Document, is used to apply for various types of travel documents, including Advance Parole, Reentry Permits, and Refugee Travel Documents. Adjustment of status applicants often use Form I-131 to request Advance Parole to travel outside the U.S. while their green card application is pending.
Instructions Summary
Use this plain-language guide to complete key sections of Form I-131 based on your travel purpose:
Part 1: Reason for Application – Choose your travel document type: Reentry Permit, Refugee Travel Document, or Advance Parole.
Part 2: Application Type – Indicate whether this is a first-time, renewal, or replacement request.
Part 3: Processing Information – Travel dates, duration, and purpose of travel.
Part 4: Information About Your Proposed Travel – List countries and purpose of visit.
Part 5: Complete Only If Applying for Advance Parole – Include information about travel plans.
Part 6: Complete Only If Applying for Reentry Permit or Refugee Travel – Past travel history and intended future use.
Part 7: Signature and Certification – Must be signed and dated.
Parts 8–10: Interpreter, Preparer, and Additional Information sections.
Completion Checklist
Use this checklist to ensure your I-131 application is complete:
Filing Tips
- Do not travel until Advance Parole is approved; leaving the U.S. early can result in abandonment of I-485.
- Always include supporting documentation, even if not explicitly required.
- Make sure travel dates do not conflict with USCIS interview timelines.
- Submit the most current version of the form and verify fees on USCIS.gov.

I-601 & I-601A Waiver Completion Guide + Checklist
Overview
Forms I-601 and I-601A are used to request a waiver of certain grounds of inadmissibility. Form I-601 is generally filed outside the U.S. after a consular interview or denial, while Form I-601A is a provisional waiver filed from within the U.S. before leaving for a consular interview. Both require demonstrating extreme hardship to a qualifying U.S. citizen or lawful permanent resident relative.
Instructions Summary
Use this summary to help complete key sections of each form. Be sure to use the correct form for the applicant’s situation.
Form I-601
Filed after a finding of inadmissibility, often after a consular interview.
Requires detailed explanation of hardship and full documentation.
Common uses: unlawful presence, misrepresentation, criminal grounds.
Form I-601A
Filed provisionally from within the U.S. by individuals planning to consular process.
Only covers unlawful presence; applicant must not have other inadmissibility grounds.
Requires proof of extreme hardship to a qualifying relative.
Common Requirements
Qualifying relative must be a U.S. citizen or LPR spouse or parent (not child).
Detailed personal statement and supporting affidavits recommended.
Include strong documentation: medical records, financial records, psychological evaluations, etc.
Completion Checklist
Use this checklist to assemble a strong and complete waiver application:
Filing Tips
- For I-601A, do not depart the U.S. until USCIS has approved the waiver.
- Explain hardship clearly and thoroughly—this is the key to approval.
- Supporting documentation should be well-organized and referenced in a cover letter.
- Track USCIS processing times online and respond promptly to any RFE (Request for Evidence).

G-28 Notice of Appearance Guide + Checklist
Overview
Form G-28, Notice of Entry of Appearance as Attorney or Accredited Representative, is used to formally notify USCIS or the immigration court that an applicant or petitioner is represented by legal counsel or an accredited representative. A properly completed G-28 ensures that all correspondence and updates are sent to the representative on record.
Instructions Summary
Below is a simple breakdown of how to complete Form G-28:
Part 1: Information About Attorney or Accredited Representative – Include full name, firm name, address, phone number, email, and bar number.
Part 2: Eligibility Information – Check appropriate boxes showing authority to practice before DHS or EOIR.
Part 3: Notice Preferences – Indicate where USCIS or EOIR should send notices and secure documents.
Part 4: Client Information – Enter the name, A-number, and contact info of the individual being represented.
Part 5: Signature of Attorney or Representative – Required in all cases.
Part 6: Signature of Applicant or Petitioner – The client must sign to authorize representation.
Completion Checklist
Use this checklist to ensure Form G-28 is complete and accurate:
Filing Tips
- Always submit a separate G-28 with each USCIS form or EOIR filing.
- Double check spelling of names and ensure addresses match across forms.
- For EOIR filings, submit the G-28 along with EOIR-27 or EOIR-28 as applicable.
- Keep a scanned copy in your digital client file for reference.

I-765 Completion Guide + Checklist
Overview
Form I-765, Application for Employment Authorization, allows eligible immigrants to request a work permit (EAD card) while their immigration case is pending or under certain categories of eligibility. This form is frequently filed with Form I-485 (Adjustment of Status) or other humanitarian applications.
Instructions Summary
Below is a plain-language guide to completing key sections of Form I-765:
Part 1: Reason for Applying – Select one box to indicate whether you are applying for initial, renewal, or replacement.
Part 2: Personal Information – Full name, address, gender, marital status, etc.
Part 2 (continued): Alien Registration Number (A#), USCIS Online Account Number, place and date of birth.
Part 2 (continued): Eligibility Category – Enter the code that matches your status (e.g., (c)(9) for I-485 applicants).
Part 2 (continued): Previous EAD Information – Include details if you’ve ever received a work permit before.
Part 2 (continued): Applicant’s Signature and Date – Sign in black ink.
Completion Checklist
Use the checklist below to ensure your I-765 submission is complete:
Filing Tips
- Always use the most current version of the form from USCIS.gov.
- Ensure photos meet USCIS specifications (2x2 inches, white background).
- Use black ink and write clearly if completing by hand.
- Double check eligibility category (e.g., (c)(3)(C) for STEM OPT).
- Submit all supporting evidence to avoid delay or rejection.

I-485 Completion Guide + Checklist
Overview
Form I-485, Application to Register Permanent Residence or Adjust Status, is used by individuals in the United States to apply for lawful permanent resident status (a green card). This form is typically filed by applicants who are eligible to adjust their status based on a family-based, employment-based, or humanitarian petition.
Instructions Summary
Below is a plain-language guide to completing each section of Form I-485:
Part 1: Information About You – Include your full name, A-Number (if any), and USCIS Online Account Number.
Part 2: Application Type or Filing Category – Select the basis for your adjustment (e.g., family, employment, asylum).
Part 3: Additional Information – Prior immigration petitions and applications.
Part 4: Address and History – Current and previous addresses, employment, travel history.
Part 5: Information About Your Parents – Names, birthdates, and locations.
Part 6: Information About Your Marital History – Marriage details and spouse information.
Part 7: Children – Include names, birthdates, and immigration status.
Part 8: General Eligibility Questions – Yes/No questions regarding immigration violations, crimes, etc.
Part 9: Public Charge – Financial support and use of public benefits.
Part 10: Applicant’s Statement, Certification, and Signature.
Parts 11–13: Interpreter, Preparer, and Additional Information sections.
Completion Checklist
Use this checklist to ensure your I-485 application packet is complete:
Filing Tips
- Double check eligibility category and filing location before submitting.
- Ensure all Yes/No questions are answered completely.
- Submit a clean and complete packet to avoid RFEs (Requests for Evidence).
- Submit medical exam (Form I-693) either with filing or bring to interview.
- If filing with I-130, include it in the same package with supporting documents.

I-589 Asylum Application Guide + Checklist
Overview
Form I-589, Application for Asylum and for Withholding of Removal, is used by individuals who are physically present in the United States and fear persecution in their home country due to race, religion, nationality, political opinion, or membership in a particular social group. This form is used to apply for both asylum and withholding of removal. It must be filed within one year of the applicant’s last arrival to the U.S., unless an exception applies.
Instructions Summary
Below is a user-friendly breakdown of the major sections in Form I-589:
Part A.I: Information About the Applicant – Personal details, A-number, address, marital status, passport info.
Part A.II: Information About the Applicant’s Family – Spouse and children in and outside the U.S.
Part B: Information About Your Background – Education, employment, military service, memberships.
Part C: Information About Your Application – Why you are applying for asylum or withholding of removal.
Part D: Additional Information About Your Application – Prior asylum filings, fear of return, etc.
Part E: Declaration of the Applicant – Signature under penalty of perjury.
Part F–H: Interpreter, Preparer, and Additional Information sections.
Completion Checklist
Use this checklist to ensure your I-589 submission is complete and compelling:
Filing Tips
- Include a compelling personal declaration – this is the heart of the asylum application.
- Be consistent across all documents and testimony.
- File within one year of your last entry unless you qualify for an exception (changed or extraordinary circumstances).
- If submitting to USCIS, do not include a filing fee; asylum applications are free.
- If in removal proceedings, file directly with the immigration court (EOIR).

I-130 Completion Guide + Checklist
Overview
Form I-130, Petition for Alien Relative, is used by U.S. citizens and lawful permanent residents to establish the qualifying relationship with certain alien relatives who wish to immigrate to the United States. This form is often the first step in helping a relative apply for lawful permanent residence.
Instructions Summary
Below is a plain-language guide to completing each section of Form I-130:
Part 1: Relationship to the Beneficiary – Identify the nature of your relationship (e.g., spouse, child, parent, sibling).
Part 2: Information About You (Petitioner) – Include your name, address, date of birth, and immigration status.
Part 3: Biographic Information – Enter ethnicity, race, height, weight, etc.
Part 4: Information About Beneficiary – Include beneficiary’s full name, marital status, current location.
Part 5: Other Information – Details about prior petitions filed.
Part 6: Petitioner’s Statement, Contact Info, Certification – Sign and date.
Part 7: Interpreter’s Information – Complete if an interpreter was used.
Part 8: Preparer’s Information – Complete if someone else prepared the form.
Part 9: Additional Information – Use this for continuation sheets.
Completion Checklist
Use the checklist below to ensure your I-130 package is complete:
Filing Tips
- Submit to the correct USCIS lockbox or address for your state.
- Check USCIS website for current filing fees and edition dates.
- Include all required documentation to avoid delays.
- If filing with I-485 (adjustment of status), submit concurrently with supporting forms.
- Make a copy of your entire packet before mailing.

Immigration Forms Master Index
This editable master index provides a quick reference for commonly used immigration forms, including form numbers, names, purposes, filing fees, and typical use cases. This guide is ideal for new immigration paralegals and legal assistants.

RFEs & Interview Prep Checklist
Request for Evidence (RFE) Response Prep
Review RFE notice and deadline
Confirm receipt in case file
Identify missing documents/information requested
Draft response letter or attorney cover letter
Gather all supporting documents
Prepare translations or certifications (if needed)
Review and proof final RFE package
Mail response with tracking and retain copy
Interview Prep for Client
Confirm interview notice and location
Review full case file with client
Practice Q&A for eligibility, relationship, and red flags
Remind client to bring all original documents and ID
Prepare updated supporting documents (if changed since filing)
Confirm interpreter if needed
Print interview appointment notice for client and attorney/paralegal
Arrive 30 minutes early with documents neatly organized

Immigration Mailing Log
Use this log to record all documents mailed to USCIS, NVC, EOIR, or other immigration-related agencies.

Immigration Client Communication Log
Log all client communications regarding immigration matters, including calls, emails, texts, and case updates.

Immigration Case Timeline Tracker
Track all major milestones and case updates using this editable timeline. Useful for USCIS, NVC, and EOIR cases.

Supporting Document Checklist
Identification Documents
Client’s Passport (all pages, including expired)
Birth Certificate (with certified translation if not in English)
Government-issued ID (Driver’s License, National ID, etc.)
Marriage Certificate / Divorce Decree (if applicable)
Immigration History Documents
I-94 Arrival/Departure Record
Visa Stamp and Entry Stamp Copies
Past USCIS Notices (I-797s, RFEs, etc.)
Removal/Deportation Orders (if any)
Proof of Relationship (for family-based petitions)
Photos together (time-stamped)
Joint lease, utility bills, bank accounts
Affidavits from friends/family
Children’s birth certificates
Financial Documents (for Affidavit of Support)
Tax Returns (last 3 years)
Pay Stubs (last 6 months)
Letter from Employer
Bank Statements
Medical / Miscellaneous Documents
Form I-693 – Report of Medical Examination
Police Clearance Letters (if required)
Certified Translations of any non-English documents

Immigration Client Intake Form

Date of Birth: ___________________________
Phone: _________________________________
Email: _________________________________
Country of Birth: _________________________
Country of Citizenship: ___________________
Immigration History

Current Immigration Status: ______________________
Previous Visa Types or Petitions Filed: ______________
Has the client ever been in removal/deportation proceedings? (Yes/No)
Family Information
Spouse’s Name (if applicable): ______________________
Children’s Names & DOBs: _________________________
Other Family in the U.S. (Names and Relation): ________
Employment & Education

Work Authorization Document: _____________________
Highest Level of Education Completed: _______________

Affidavit of Support (I-864) Prep Sheet
Sponsor Information
Sponsor's Full Name: ____________________________
Sponsor's Date of Birth: _________________________
Sponsor's Address: _____________________________
U.S. Citizen or Lawful Permanent Resident: _______
Relationship to Beneficiary: ______________________
Household Size (for Poverty Guidelines)
Sponsor
Spouse (if counted)
Children (if counted)
Other dependents or co-sponsors
Total Household Size: _________
Income & Employment
Current Employer: ________________________________
Annual Income: $________________
Filed Federal Tax Returns for Past 3 Years? (Y/N): __
Using Joint Sponsor? (Y/N): _______________________
Required Supporting Documents
Most recent federal tax return (Form 1040)
W-2s or 1099s for the last year
Letter from employer verifying income
Proof of citizenship or lawful permanent resident status
Proof of current income (pay stubs)

USCIS Communication Tracker
Track all correspondence with USCIS including receipt notices, RFEs, NOIDs, and other case-related notices.

USCIS Form Filing Checklist
Common Family-Based Forms
I-130 – Petition for Alien Relative
I-485 – Application to Register Permanent Residence or Adjust Status
I-765 – Application for Employment Authorization
I-131 – Application for Travel Document (Advance Parole)
I-864 – Affidavit of Support
Naturalization & Citizenship Forms
N-400 – Application for Naturalization
N-600 – Application for Certificate of Citizenship
Humanitarian & Asylum Forms
I-589 – Application for Asylum and for Withholding of Removal
I-918 – Petition for U Nonimmigrant Status (U Visa)
I-914 – Application for T Nonimmigrant Status (T Visa)
I-601 – Waiver of Grounds of Inadmissibility
Employment & Business-Based Forms
I-140 – Immigrant Petition for Alien Worker
I-129 – Petition for a Nonimmigrant Worker
I-907 – Request for Premium Processing Service
Notes / Special Instructions
Always include appropriate filing fees and checks made out to 'U.S. Department of Homeland Security'.
Verify edition dates of all forms before filing.
Make copies of all forms and supporting documents prior to mailing.

Immigration Fee & Payment Tracker
Use this tracker to manage USCIS filing fees, payment details, and any requests for fee waivers or refunds.
